text: Annealing the air-melted material to temperatures in excess of $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ brought about significant reductions in the minimum fracture strain exhibited by the alloy.
Holding at temperatures of about $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for an extended period recovered the fracture ductility.
Aging material in the 1100 to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ range that had been previously annealed at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ brought about a significant reduction in the ductility.

This alloy is nickel-base and was chosen for use in the MSR because it offered good resistance to corrosion by molten-fluorides salts and possessed moderate mechanical strength. $^{1}$ The basic properties of this alloy have been investigated and reported previously. $^{2}$ However, the use of numerous heats of this alloy under various sets of circumstances has revealed potential problem areas.

Most of the tensile tests were run in a hydraulic Baldwin testing machine at a crosshead speed of 0.05 in./min or a strain rate of $2.5\%/\min$ .
A standard equilibrating time of $1/2\mathrm{hr}$ was used for all specimens to reach the desired test temperature.
The tensile properties of heat 5075, after annealing 1 hr at 2150 and $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , are given in Table 2 and depicted graphically in Figs.

Both the tensile and yield strengths were lower for the material annealed at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ than for the material annealed at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ except at test temperatures of $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ where the reverse was noted.
After annealing at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the material exhibits a ductility minimum in the temperature range of 1200 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ anneal shifts the ductility minimum to $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and reduces the minimum ductility significantly.
However, the ductility below $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is not very different for material annealed at 2150 and $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The tensile properties of heat 5074 after annealing at 2150 and $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are given in Table 3.
The tensile and yield strengths of heat 5074 are comparable with those of heat 5075.
After annealing at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the ductility minimum of heat 5074 occurs between 1400 and $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
After annealing at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the fracture ductility is still decreasing with temperature at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
However, the ductility below $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is not significantly different for material annealed at 2150 and $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The properties of heat 5073 in longitudinal and transverse orientations (with respect to rolling direction) are given in Table 4.
The strength variations with test temperature and heat treatment are comparable with those just discussed for heats 5074 and 5075.

After annealing at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the fracture ductility at lower temperatures is not changed significantly, but it decreases very rapidly above $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The ductility continues to decrease through the highest temperature investigated, $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , where a reduction in area of only $1.6\%$ was obtained.

The tensile properties of a heat of vacuum-melted Hastelloy N, heat 2477, are given in Table 6.
The first two tests listed in the table indicate that the mill anneal probably did not actually reach $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The ductility of the mill-annealed material goes through several fluctuations as a function of test temperature but the reduction in area never goes below $35\%$ .
A 1-hr anneal at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ slightly reduces the ductility at high temperatures but the minimum reduction in area observed is $26\%$
aAnnealed 1 hr at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in argon, fast cooled.
cAnnealed 1 hr at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in argon, fast cooled.
Tensile curves at $75^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are quite smooth and appear normal.
Curves at 800 to $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ exhibit serrations equivalent to as much as 4000 psi and occur at frequencies as rapid as 1 serration/0.025% strain.

At $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the curves are usually quite smooth; the material shows some strain hardening, reaches its maximum load at a very low strain, and then the load continues to decrease during the rest of the test.

Since air-melted Hastelloy N exhibits good ductility after annealing at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and much lower ductility after annealing at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , several tests were run to determine how rapidly the ductility decreased with increasing annealing temperature.
The results of these tests are given in Table 7.
Specimens were annealed 1 hr at temperatures between 2150 and $2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and were tested at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , a temperature in the minimum ductility range.
As shown in Table 7, the ductility is significantly reduced by an anneal at $2200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ over that observed after a $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ anneal.

Annealing at $2250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ brings about a further reduction in ductility, but increasing the annealing temperature to 2300 and $2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ does not result in further embrittlement.

Since the fracture ductility of the air-melted Hastellloy N was rendered quite low by pretest annealing at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , several notched specimens were tested to determine the influence of a sharp notch on the fracture ductility.
The results of these tests are summarized in Table 8.
The notch was 0.030-in. deep and had a root radius of 0.001 to 0.0015 in. and an included angle of $30^{\circ}$ at the base.
4, indicate that, after the material has been annealed at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , fracture can occur at a notch with no measurable plastic strain.

Figures 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 illustrate that microstructure of heat 5075 after annealing 1 hr at 2050, 2200, 2250, 2300, 2400, and $2500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , respectively.
These stringers do not appear to dissolve at an appreciable rate at temperatures less than $2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
At $2500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the discrete precipitate particles have dissolved, but a lamellar product is present in the grain boundaries.
There is also some evidence of melting during the $2500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ anneal and the inhomogeneous distribution of the molten areas illustrates the inhomogeneity of this material.
The grain sizes are equivalent after anneals at 2150 and $2200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The grain size is increased by an anneal at $2250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ although the grain growth is reduced significantly in areas where the stringers are present.
Annealing at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ does not result in a significantly larger grain size than that obtained at $2250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The influence of the stringers on the grain growth has disappeared at $2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the grain size is quite large.

Annealing at $2500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ does not result in additional grain growth, but the grain boundary layer and precipitate particles appear to be converted to an intergranular lamellar product (Fig.
Heats 5074, 5073, and SP-25 were also annealed at various temperatures.
The resulting structures were quite similar to those just described for heat 5075.
However, the structure of heat 2477 was quite different.
Figures 11, 12, 13, 14, and 15 illustrate the structure of this material after annealing 1 hr at 2150, 2200, 2300, 2400, and $2500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , respectively.
After annealing at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the structure contains randomly dispersed precipitates rather than the stringers found in heats 5073, 5074, 5075,

Annealing at $2200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ causes some grain growth and solution of a significant number of the precipitates.
A $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ anneal causes some additional grain growth and further solution of precipitate.
A $2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ anneal produces a structure which appears to be single phase with a few inclusions present.
Annealing at $2500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ produces further grain growth but no intergranular voids indicative of melting are produced.
Figure 16 shows the fracture of a specimen annealed at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and tested at $75^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The structure resulting from a test at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is quite similar with the exception that more cracks are present.
Testing at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ resulted in failure by combined intergranular and transgranular modes.
Testing at $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ produces an intergranular fracture and numerous intergranular cracks.

Although all of the microstructures used to illustrate the structure of tested specimens have been of heat 5075, the structures of heats 5073, 5074, and SP-25 were quite similar with only one noteworthy exception.
The data in Tables 2, 3, 4, and 5 show that some heats under certain conditions went through a ductility minimum and then exhibited good ductility at 1600 to $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
To illustrate, heat 5075 exhibited good ductility at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ after having been annealed at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the microstructure in Fig.
18 indicates that recrystallization occurred.
Heat 5073T, after being annealed at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , continued to show decreasing ductility with temperature at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and Fig.
23 indicates that recrystallization did not occur during test.
Heat 5074 also exhibited poor ductility at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ after a $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ anneal and was found not to be recrystallized.
However, after a $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ anneal, heat 5074 was ductile at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and Fig.
24 shows that recrystallization did occur during the test.
Tested specimens of heat 2477 (vacuum melted) exhibited structures quite similar to those of the other heats at temperatures up to about $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Figure 25 shows the microstructure of a specimen annealed at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and tested at $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The reduction in area of this specimen was about $30\%$ .
Figure 26 shows the microstructure of a specimen annealed at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and tested at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The reduction in area was $39\%$ .

The results shown in Table 9 indicate that the fracture ductility is improved by reducing the cooling rate from the pretest annealing temperature of $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Typical results are shown in Table 10.
The improvement in fracture ductility brought about by prestraining at $75^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is quite significant.

Since annealing studies had shown that all of the precipitate could be dissolved in heat 2477, the possibility of embrittlement as a result of reprecipitation was considered.
Specimens were annealed at $2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and aged for 100 hr at 1500, 1700, and $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
At $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the structure shown in Fig.
At $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , some precipitation occurred along twin boundaries, and at $1900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ no visible reprecipitation occurred.
The results of these tests are summarized in Table 12.
Aging for 100 hr at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ had very little, if any, effect on the tensile properties.
However, cold working the specimens prior to aging at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ increased the strength by an amount which increased with increasing cold working.
The resulting structure after annealing at $2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , cold working $10\%$ , and testing at $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is shown in Fig.
The precipitate formed under these conditions is too fine to be resolved at a magnification of 1000 diameters.

Aging at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ after annealed at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ reduces the ductility by almost a factor of 2 over a period of 1000 hr.
Figure 32 shows the microstructure of a specimen annealed at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , aged 1000 hr at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and tested at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
17 for an unaged specimen.
Aging at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ produces a decrease in the ductility by about a factor of 2, but the required aging times are much less than at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
After 1000 hr at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the material has apparently overaged and the fracture ductility is quite high.
Figure 33 is a photomicrograph of a specimen annealed at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , aged 1000 hr at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and tested at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
If the material is initially annealed at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and aged at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for 1000 hr, no apparent change in properties occurs.
However, aging at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for 1000 hr results in a very high fracture ductility.
Heat 5075 gave results very similar to those of heat 5074.
After annealing at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , aging at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ reduced the fracture ductility.
Aging for 100 hr at 1400, 1600, and $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ did not significantly alter the fracture ductility.
After annealing at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , it was possible to improve the ductility by aging at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ prior to testing at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
As can be seen from the data in Table 13, the ductility becomes progressively better as the aging time at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ increases.
Figure 34 shows the fracture of a specimen annealed at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , aged for 100 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and tested at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Annealing at temperatures in excess of $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ lowers the fracture ductility at test temperatures in excess of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

and then good ductility at higher temperatures, whereas the ductility of other heats continues to decrease with increasing temperature through $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the maximum temperature investigated.
The air-melted heats showed reductions in area at fractures of less than $2\%$ under some conditions.
The vacuum-melted material was more ductile with a minimum reduction in area of $26\%$ being obtained.
These particles were identified as $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ type carbides.

As the pretest annealing temperature was increased above $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , some grain growth occurred, the precipitate particles began to dissolve, and the grain boundaries became broader and etched more readily.
At $2500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the precipitate particles were completely dissolved and a lamellar grain boundary phase appeared.
To determine whether the lamellar phase formed directly from the $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ precipitates, the end of a wire was induction melted and cooled very rapidly.
35 shows the transformation taking place.
Hence, it is not known whether it has a stoichiometric ratio different from $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ or whether it simply represents another distribution of the same phase.
The vacuum-melted material appeared quite similar to that of the air-melted material after annealing at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
However, the carbides appear to dissolve more rapidly in the vacuum-melted material and a single-phase microstructure is obtained by annealing at $2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Further studies have shown that after Hastelloy N has been annealed at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , which should produce a very low minimum fracture ductility, it is possible to recover the ductility by several techniques.
Also, aging at a temperature in the 1600 to $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ range for a few hours seems
(a) $100 \times$ , (b) $500 \times$ .
The ability to recover ductility after annealing at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ by aging at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ has already been pointed out.
There is also a tendency to embrittle Hastelloy N after a $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ anneal by annealing in the 1100 to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ range.
The minimum ductility can be lowered by a factor of 2.
If a precipitate is formed during aging, it is too fine to be resolved at $1000 \times 10^{4}$ .
Glen, $^{5}$ for example, has shown that various strain aging effects occur during tensile testing of steels, each associated with the presence of a particular alloying element.
Hastelloy N exhibits serrated stress-strain curves over the temperature range of 800 to $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and often small maxima in yield strength occur in the same temperature range.
Rhines and Wray<sup>4</sup> have attributed brittleness in nickel to the occurrence of grain boundary shearing and associated intergranular rupture.
Smith<sup>6</sup> also looked at the ductility minimum in nickel.

Although he concurred with Rhines on several points, he felt that the intergranular voids were nucleated at grain boundary voids by a process proposed by Gifkins<sup>7</sup> which involved the formation of grain boundary jogs due to slip steps.
Bieber and Decker<sup>8</sup> have investigated the effects of a number of minor constituents on the ductility of nickel.
Additions of several elements in concentrations up to 0.5 wt % were made and their influence on the rupture ductility evaluated.
Noticeable loss of ductility between 1000 and $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was reported for additions of as little as 0.0005 wt % of some of the impurities.
Olsen et al.<sup>9</sup> observed a minimum in the ductility vs heat-treating temperature curve for cold-worked and recrystallized nickel containing 0.0009 wt % sulfur.
A slightly different impurity mechanism has been proposed to account for the ductility of Nimonic 80A.10 During aging, a chromium-rich carbide is formed in the grain boundary.
Apparently, temperatures above $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ result in the formation of this brittle grain boundary layer.
Aging studies on material annealed at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ indicate that this grain boundary layer can also be developed by aging in the 1100 to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ range.

Four heats of air-melted material were found to exhibit quite low fracture strains in the 1400 to $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ range after annealing at temperatures in excess of $2200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Aging at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , very slow cooling rates, and cold working were effective in recovering the ductility.
The recovery of good ductility at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in some heats was associated with recrystallization.
After annealing at $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , it was found that the high-temperature ductility was reduced by aging in the 1100 to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ range.
